audit_trail.notes: All three reviewers across both cycles confirmed that the review bundle contains only the abstract, author affiliations, journal metadata, and reference list; the Article Text section heading is present but no body content follows. The paper is published open-access (CC BY 4.0) in Phys. Rev. Research, so the extraction failure is a pipeline issue, not a paywall issue. Gemini's zero/near-zero scores were based on treating the extraction failure as fatal to the paper itself; Claude and Codex rejected this interpretation, and the mechanical average was used as the selected final score. In cycle 2, Claude elevated gauge-invariance to an explicit technical objection (prompted by Codex) and added the renormalization prescription as an explicit missing assumption; Codex added non-Gaussianity to missing assumptions. No reviewer changed their overall verdict between cycles. The dominant epistemic limitation for this synthesis is the unavailability of the full article body for technical audit.

general_reader_takeaway: This paper proposes a new theory of cosmic inflation that eliminates the need for the hypothetical "inflaton" particle — instead, it aims to show that gravitational waves already present in quantum vacuum fluctuations can generate the density variations seeding all large-scale structure. The idea is ambitious and conceptually fresh, and it passed peer review at a reputable journal. However, the review board could not audit the key calculations because the full article text was not captured in the review bundle, and the abstract alone faces serious unresolved theoretical challenges — most notably, why second-order effects from gravitational waves would produce scalar perturbations far larger than those waves themselves, which naive counting says should not happen.
human_spotchecks: Retrieve and read the full article text (open-access under CC BY 4.0 at https://journals.aps.org/prresearch/abstract/10.1103/vfny-pgc2) to audit the derivation of the induced scalar power spectrum — this is the single most important unresolved question.

Verify whether the paper resolves the amplitude hierarchy: specifically, what explicit parameter regime or enhancement mechanism allows second-order tensor-induced scalars to dominate first-order tensor modes while simultaneously satisfying Planck 2018 constraints on A_s, n_s, and r.

Check whether the gauge-invariant scalar variable is defined carefully at second order (e.g., whether the reported spectrum corresponds to the gauge-invariant curvature perturbation ζ or R, and whether the second-order gauge transformation is handled correctly).
Determine what drives the background de Sitter phase and how the inflationary Hubble scale H is fixed — whether this reintroduces effective model-dependence comparable to an inflaton potential.

Assess what role, if any, the de Sitter instability frameworks (Mottola Refs. 21–22, 26–27; Dvali-Gomez Refs. 24–25; Alicki-Barenboim-Jenkins Refs. 28–29) play as load-bearing steps in the argument, and whether results are robust if those contested frameworks are removed.
Check whether the paper addresses non-Gaussianity predictions (bispectrum, f_NL) of the second-order generation mechanism and whether they are consistent with Planck observational limits.
Verify that the transition from de Sitter to radiation domination is dynamically specified (matching conditions, radiation production) rather than simply assumed.
public_summary: Bertacca, Jimenez, Matarrese, and Ricciardone (Phys. Rev. Research 7, L032010, July 2025) propose an inflationary scenario in which no scalar inflaton field is required. The paper argues that a pure de Sitter spacetime, driven by some unspecified source of vacuum energy, naturally produces first-order gravitational wave (tensor) fluctuations from quantum vacuum oscillations; scalar curvature perturbations — the seeds of all observed large-scale cosmic structure — then arise at second order through nonlinear tensor-tensor coupling. The authors claim the resulting scalar power spectrum is near scale-invariant, that conditions exist under which scalar amplitude greatly exceeds tensor amplitude (consistent with observed bounds on the tensor-to-scalar ratio r), and that inflation ends naturally through a transition to a radiation-dominated phase. The mathematical ingredients used — second-order cosmological perturbation theory and gravitational-wave-induced scalar contributions — are well-established in the literature, with key prior work by Matarrese, Mollerach, and Bruni (1998) and by an overlapping author group (Bari et al. 2022–2023). The genuine novelty is promoting these second-order tensor effects from a subdominant correction to the primary mechanism for generating the observed scalar spectrum. The paper is published as a Letter in Phys. Rev. Research under an open-access CC BY 4.0 license, providing a meaningful signal of peer-reviewed acceptance. However, the review board's technical audit was severely curtailed: the full article body was not captured in the review bundle (confirmed by all three reviewers across both cycles), limiting evaluation to the abstract and 36-item reference list. The central quantitative claims therefore remain unverified by this review.
reviewer_disagreements: Scoring weight of the missing body text: Gemini assigned scores of 0/10 (cycle 1) and 1/10 (cycle 2) and a 'major_flaw' verdict, treating the extraction failure as effectively fatal to the paper; Claude (5/10, 'promising') and Codex (4/10, 'weak_evidence') held that the failure is in the review infrastructure rather than in the published article, and that peer acceptance at Phys. Rev. Research is a meaningful positive signal that cannot be discounted.

Gemini's reviewer_confidence dimension score of 10/10 in cycle 1 (certainty about a paper based solely on missing text) was rejected by Claude as epistemically unjustified; Claude argued that certainty about quality cannot be derived from the absence of content.

Claude assessed the scenario as 'promising' conditional on the full text holding up; Codex preferred 'weak_evidence' on the grounds that unresolved proof/computation gaps across all core claims (amplitude hierarchy, gauge-invariance, exit dynamics, non-Gaussianity) are too numerous to award a positive verdict even provisionally.

strongest_objections.objection: The full article body was not extracted into the review bundle — only the abstract and reference list are present. No derivations, equations, lemmas, or proofs can be audited. This is a failure of bundle extraction, not of the paper itself, but it renders all technical claims formally unverifiable by this review.
strongest_objections.paper_location: Entire Article Text section (heading present, no content follows)

strongest_objections.objection: Amplitude hierarchy problem: second-order scalar perturbations sourced by tensor modes are generically O(h²) relative to the tensor amplitude O(h). For Planck-normalized scalars (A_s ≈ 2.1×10⁻⁹), naive scaling predicts the tensor-to-scalar ratio r ≫ 1, directly contradicting the observational bound r < 0.036. The abstract claims scalar perturbations can be 'much larger than tensor modes' but provides no hint of the enhancement mechanism required to invert this hierarchy.
strongest_objections.paper_location: Abstract (claim that scalar perturbations become 'much larger than tensor modes')

strongest_objections.objection: Near scale-invariance of the second-order scalar power spectrum is not automatic. The induced scalar spectrum is a convolution of the tensor spectrum with itself, weighted by Green's function kernels; known results from induced-GW / induced-curvature calculations (Mollerach-Harari-Matarrese 2004; Ananda-Clarkson-Wands 2007) show that this generically produces non-trivial spectral features and tilt rather than n_s ≈ 0.965.

Gauge-invariance at second order is not addressed in the abstract. Second-order scalar perturbations are sensitive to gauge choice; without specifying the gauge-invariant scalar variable (such as the curvature perturbation ζ or R), the claimed power spectrum may partly reflect gauge artifacts rather than physical observables.
strongest_objections.paper_location: Abstract (claim about the computed scalar power spectrum)

strongest_objections.objection: The graceful exit mechanism from de Sitter inflation to radiation domination is asserted to be 'natural' but is not technically described in the accessible text. Ending inflation without a rolling scalar field and without fine-tuning the transition moment is a known difficulty, and the abstract provides no matching conditions, radiation production mechanism, or timescale.
strongest_objections.paper_location: Abstract (claim of a natural mechanism to end inflation)

strongest_objections.objection: Non-Gaussianity predictions are entirely absent from the abstract. Second-order scalar generation from tensor modes generically produces non-Gaussian signatures; Planck bounds on the bispectrum (|f_NL| of order a few) tightly constrain second-order mechanisms. It is unknown whether this observable is addressed in the full paper.

strongest_supported_claims: The mathematical framework of second-order scalar perturbation generation from tensor modes is established in the literature (Matarrese-Mollerach-Bruni 1998; Bari et al. 2022–2023, Refs. 18–20 in the paper), providing a rigorous starting point for the proposed mechanism.

The conceptual step of promoting second-order tensor-induced scalars from a subdominant correction to the primary source of primordial structure is genuinely novel as a complete inflationary scenario, and is not present in the cited prior work.

The paper passed peer review at Phys. Rev. Research (Letter format, July 2025), providing an external quality signal that the calculation was found credible by independent referees with access to the full text.

The paper correctly identifies that near scale-invariance of the tensor spectrum in de Sitter (n_T = 0) is a natural starting point; the non-trivial question of whether the induced scalar spectrum inherits this flatness is acknowledged as something the paper claims to derive.

The reference list includes Ref. 31 (Negro-Patil, Hadamard regularization of the graviton stress tensor), indicating the authors are aware of the renormalization subtleties involved in quantum tensor fluctuations in de Sitter space.
technical_takeaway: The paper claims that in a pure de Sitter background, primordial scalar curvature perturbations can be generated at second order in tensor metric fluctuations (gravitational waves from quantum vacuum), bypassing any scalar inflaton field, and that the induced scalar power spectrum is near scale-invariant with a parameter regime yielding r ≪ 1. All three reviewers confirm that only the abstract and reference list were extracted into the bundle; no derivations, lemmas, or equations were auditable. The dominant unresolved concern — raised independently by all reviewers — is the amplitude hierarchy: second-order induced scalars are generically O(h²) relative to first-order tensors O(h), making scalar dominance over tensors non-trivial to achieve without an explicit enhancement mechanism. Additional open questions include gauge-invariance of the second-order scalar variable, near scale-invariance of a convolution-derived spectrum, the graceful exit mechanism, and non-Gaussianity predictions.
